title: Cat Swollen Paw: Causes, Urgency, and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat's paw twice its normal size? Learn about causes like abscesses or stings, the urgency level, and how photos help veterinary triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: A significantly swollen paw usually indicates infection or injury and requires veterinary care within 12 to 24 hours. Seek immediate emergency help if your cat is lethargic, not eating, or the limb feels cold.

What are the common causes for my cat's paw to swell?

  • Abscesses resulting from bites or scratches
  • Allergic reactions to insect stings
  • Foreign bodies such as thorns or splinters embedded in the pad
  • Structural injuries like fractures or severe sprains
  • Plasma cell pododermatitis (also known as pillow paw)

When should I take my cat to the vet for a swollen paw?

  • Schedule a veterinary visit within 12 to 24 hours for localized swelling and pain.
  • Seek immediate emergency care if your cat is lethargic, hiding, or refusing to eat.
  • Go to an emergency clinic immediately if the affected limb feels cold to the touch.

How does sending a photo of the swollen paw help my veterinarian?

  • Allows professionals to identify visible puncture wounds or drainage.
  • Helps distinguish specific redness patterns, such as an insect sting versus a deep infection.
  • Enables the triage team to determine how quickly your cat needs to be seen.
  • Helps the clinic prepare for necessary diagnostic steps before you arrive.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Arterial thromboembolism, often secondary to conditions causing left atrial enlargement such as cardiomyopathies or hyperthyroidism, can result in swollen paws in cats. Thrombi form in the left atrium and embolize, commonly affecting the aortic trifurcation. Clinical signs include pain, paresis or paralysis of the hindlimbs, and reduced or absent arterial pulses (femoral or pedal) in the affected limbs. The affected limbs are often cooler than normal, with firm, swollen gastrocnemius muscle bellies. Diagnosis is based on clinical signs, physical examination findings, and Doppler blood flow readings. Ultrasound can be used to identify the thromboembolus.

Chapter: Cardiology, Neurology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 143)

faqs.question: Should I try to drain the swelling at home?
faqs.answer: No, you should never attempt to lance or drain a swollen paw yourself. Doing so can cause extreme pain, risk a deeper infection, and may lead to a bite or scratch from your stressed pet.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human pain relievers?
faqs.answer: Absolutely not. Many human medications, such as Ibuprofen or Acetaminophen (Tylenol), are highly toxic to cats and can be fatal even in small doses.

faqs.question: How can I keep my cat comfortable until the vet visit?
faqs.answer: Keep your cat indoors in a quiet, confined space to limit their movement. You can try a cool compress if they tolerate it, but do not force it if it causes them distress.
